Consider the following grammar where $S$ is the start symbol, and $a$ and $b$ are terminal symbols.

$$ S \rightarrow a S b S|b S| \in $$

Which of the following statements is/are true?

options.content: The string $a b b$ has two distinct derivations in this grammar.
The string $a b a b$ has only one rightmost derivation.
The language generated by the grammar is undecidable.

explanation:
$$ S \rightarrow a S b S|b S| \in $$

$a b b$ has 2 different parse trees.

$\therefore \quad$ Grammar is ambiguous.

$$ \begin{aligned} S & \rightarrow a \underline{S} b s \\ & \rightarrow a \in b \underline{S} \\ & \rightarrow a b b \underline{S} \\ & \rightarrow a b b \in \\ & \rightarrow a b b \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} S & \rightarrow a \underline{S} b s \\ & \rightarrow a b \underline{S} b S \\ & \rightarrow a b \in b \underline{S} \\ & \rightarrow a b b \in \\ & \rightarrow a b b \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { The string } a b b \text { has } 2 \text { distinct derivations. } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} S & \rightarrow a S b \underline{S} \\ & \rightarrow a S b a S b \underline{S} \\ & \rightarrow a S b a \underline{S} b \in \\ & \rightarrow a \underline{S} b a \in b \\ & \rightarrow a \in b a b \\ & \rightarrow a b a b \end{aligned}\\ &\therefore \quad a b a b \text { has only one right most derivation. } \end{aligned} $$

content: Which ONE of the following languages is accepted by a deterministic pushdown automaton?

options.content: Any language accepted by a non-deterministic pushdown automaton.

explanation:
Let's break down why Option A is correct:

A deterministic pushdown automaton (DPDA) is a machine that reads input and uses a stack, but its moves are completely determined by its current state, the input symbol, and the top stack symbol—there is no ambiguity or guessing.

Any regular language can be recognized by a finite automaton. Since finite automata are a special case of pushdown automata (where the stack isn't used at all), every regular language is also accepted by some DPDA.

The other options are not true:

Option B: Not all context-free languages are deterministic. Some context-free languages require non-deterministic pushdown automata to be recognized.

Option C: Non-deterministic pushdown automata (NPDA) can recognize languages that DPDAs cannot.

Option D: Decidable languages include languages that are not context-free, and some of these cannot be recognized by any pushdown automaton.

Therefore, the only guaranteed case is Option A: Any regular language.

content: Let $G_1, G_2$ be Context Free Grammars (CFGs) and $R$ be a regular expression. For a grammar $G$, let $L(G)$ denote the language generated by $G$. Which ONE among the following questions is decidable?
explanation:
We want to decide which one of the following problems is decidable when dealing with context‐free grammars (CFGs) and regular expressions:

$$L(G_1) = L(G_2) \, ?$$

$$L(G_1) \cap L(G_2) = \varnothing \, ?$$

$$L(G_1) = L(R) \, ?$$

$$L(G_1) = \varnothing \, ?$$

Let’s briefly discuss each:

• Option A: Checking if two CFGs generate the same language (i.e. equivalence of CFGs) is a classic undecidable problem.

• Option B: Deciding whether the intersection of two context‐free languages is empty is undecidable. (In general, even though emptiness is decidable for one CFG, the intersection of two CFLs need not be context‐free, and the problem turns out to be undecidable.)

• Option C: Determining whether a CFG’s language equals the language described by a regular expression is undecidable. In fact, note that if we set $$R = \Sigma^*$$ (which is regular), then Option C asks whether $$L(G_1) = \Sigma^*$$ – the universality problem for CFGs, which is known to be undecidable.

• Option D: Checking whether the language of a CFG is empty (that is, $$L(G_1) = \varnothing$$) is decidable. There is a standard algorithm that, given $$G_1,$$ marks nonterminals that can derive terminal strings and then checks if the start symbol is marked.

Thus, the decidable question is:

Option D: Is $$L(G_1)=\varnothing$$?

content:
Consider the two lists List-I and List-II given below:

List - I List - II
(i) Context free languages (a) Closed under union
(ii) Recursive languages (b) Not closed under complementation
(iii) Regular languages (c) Closed under intersection

For matching of items in List-I with those in List-II, which of the following option(s) is/ are CORRECT?
explanation:
Let’s look at the closure properties of each language class:

Context‐free languages (CFLs) are known to be closed under union but are not closed under complementation. Since the property “Not closed under complementation” (item (b)) uniquely distinguishes CFLs from the other two classes, we must pair (i) with (b).

Both Recursive languages and Regular languages enjoy all the “nice” closure properties (closed under union, intersection, and complementation). That is, for:

Recursive languages, it is true that they are closed under union as well as intersection.

Regular languages are also closed under both union and intersection.

Since items (a) “Closed under union” and (c) “Closed under intersection” are valid for both Recursive and Regular languages, we can assign them in either order. In particular, we have two acceptable matchings:

• Option B:

  (i) Context‐free languages – (b) Not closed under complementation

  (ii) Recursive languages – (a) Closed under union

  (iii) Regular languages – (c) Closed under intersection

• Option C:

  (i) Context‐free languages – (b) Not closed under complementation

  (ii) Recursive languages – (c) Closed under intersection

  (iii) Regular languages – (a) Closed under union

Both choices are correct because for Recursive languages and Regular languages, both (a) and (c) are valid closure properties.

Thus, the correct answer is that both Option B and Option C are correct.

content:
Consider the following context-free grammar $G$, where $S, A$, and $B$ are the variables (nonterminals), $a$ and $b$ are the terminal symbols, $S$ is the start variable, and the rules of $G$ are described as:

$$\begin{aligned} & S \rightarrow a a B \mid A b b \\ & A \rightarrow a \mid a A \\ & B \rightarrow b \mid b B \end{aligned}$$

Which ONE of the languages $L(G)$ is accepted by $G$ ?

options.content: $L(G)=\left\{a^n b^{2 n} \mid n \geq 1\right\} \cup\left\{a^{2 n} b^n \mid n \geq 1\right\}$

explanation:
Let's analyze the grammar step by step.

The grammar has the following productions:

$$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow aaB \mid Abb,\\[1mm] A &\rightarrow a \mid aA,\\[1mm] B &\rightarrow b \mid bB. \end{aligned} $$

We'll examine each production rule for the start symbol $ S $:

For $ S \rightarrow aaB $:

The string always starts with exactly two $ a $'s (i.e., $ aa $).

The nonterminal $ B $ generates at least one $ b $ since:

$ B \rightarrow b $ produces a single $ b $,

$ B \rightarrow bB $ allows further $ b $'s.

Resulting strings: $ aa $ followed by one or more $ b $'s, i.e.,

$$ \{a^2 b^n \mid n \geq 1\}. $$

For $ S \rightarrow Abb $:

The nonterminal $ A $ generates one or more $ a $'s:

$ A \rightarrow a $ produces a single $ a $,

$ A \rightarrow aA $ allows additional $ a $'s.

After generating the $ a $'s, the production appends exactly two $ b $'s.

Resulting strings: One or more $ a $'s, ending with $ bb $, i.e.,

$$ \{a^n b^2 \mid n \geq 1\}. $$

Now, since the grammar $ G $ can derive strings from either of these two branches, the language $ L(G) $ accepted by $ G $ is the union of the two sets:

$$ L(G) = \{a^2 b^n \mid n \geq 1\} \cup \{a^n b^2 \mid n \geq 1\}. $$

Comparing this with the provided options:

Option A: $ L(G)=\{a^2 b^n \mid n \geq 1\} \cup\{a^n b^2 \mid n \geq 1\} $

Option B: $ L(G)=\{a^n b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\} \cup\{a^{2n} b^n \mid n \geq 1\} $

Option C: $ L(G)=\{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\} $

Option D: $ L(G)=\{a^{2n} b^{2n} \mid n \geq 1\} $

Clearly, the derived language matches Option A.

Thus, the answer is:

Option A.

content: Let L<sub>1</sub> be a regular language and L<sub>2</sub> be a context-free language. Which of the following languages is/are context-free?
explanation:
Given :

$L_1$ is a regular language

$L_2$ is a context- free language.

Consider each option one by one :

Option (A) : $L_1 \cap \bar{L}_2$

Since CFLs are not closed under complementation, $\bar{L}_2$ need not be CFL. Therefore, $L_1 \cap \bar{L}_2$ may or may not be CFL.

Option (B) : $\overline{\bar{L}_1 \cup \bar{L}_2}$

$$ \overline{\bar{L}_1 \cup \bar{L}_2}=\overline{\overline{L_1}} \cap \overline{\overline{L_2}}=L_1 \cap L_2 $$

Since CFLs are closed under intersection with regular language. Therefore, $L_1 \cap L_2$ is CFL.

Option (C) :

$$ \begin{aligned} & L_1 \cup\left(L_2 \cup \bar{L}_2\right) \\ & L_1 \cup\left(L_2 \cup \bar{L}_2\right)=L_1 \cup\left(\Sigma^*\right) \\ & L_1 \cup\left(L_2 \cup \bar{L}_2\right)=\Sigma^* \end{aligned} $$

Since $\Sigma^*$ is regular, hence CFL too,

$L_1 \cup\left(L_2 \cup \bar{L}_2\right)$ is CFL.

Option (D) :

$$ \begin{aligned} & \left(L_1 \cap L_2\right) \cup\left(\bar{L}_2 \cap L_2\right) \\ & \left(L_1 \cap L_2\right) \cup\left(\bar{L}_1 \cap L_2\right)=\left(L_1 \cup \bar{L}_1\right) \cap L_2 \\ & \left(L_1 \cap L_2\right) \cup\left(\bar{L}_1 \cap L_2\right)=L_2 \end{aligned} $$

Since $L_2$ is CFL, therefore,

$$ \left(L_1 \cap L_2\right) \cup\left(\bar{L}_1 \cap L_2\right) \text { is CFL. } $$

Hence the correct option are (B), (C) and (D).

content: Suppose that L<sub>1</sub> is a regular and L<sub>2</sub> is a context-free language, Which one of the following languages is NOT necessarily context-free?
explanation:
Given:

$ L_1 $ is a regular language (RL).

$ L_2 $ is a context-free language (CFL).

Option A: $ L_1 \cdot L_2 $ (Concatenation)

Analysis:

Closure Property:

CFLs are closed under concatenation with regular languages.

This means that if you concatenate a regular language with a context-free language, the result is always a context-free language.

Conclusion:

$ L_1 \cdot L_2 $ is necessarily a context-free language.

Option B: $ L_1 \cup L_2 $ (Union)

Analysis:

Closure Property:

CFLs are closed under union with regular languages.

The union of a regular language and a context-free language is always a context-free language.

Conclusion:

$ L_1 \cup L_2 $ is necessarily a context-free language.

Option C: $ L_1 \cap L_2 $ (Intersection)

Analysis:

Closure Property:

CFLs are not closed under intersection in general.

However, the intersection of a regular language and a context-free language is always a context-free language.

This is because a deterministic finite automaton (representing the regular language) can be combined with a pushdown automaton (representing the CFL) to create a new pushdown automaton representing the intersection.

Conclusion:

$ L_1 \cap L_2 $ is necessarily a context-free language.

Option D: $ L_1 - L_2 $ (Set Difference)

Analysis:

Understanding $ L_1 - L_2 $:

$ L_1 - L_2 = L_1 \cap \overline{L_2} $

Here, $ \overline{L_2} $ is the complement of $ L_2 $.

Closure Properties Involved:

Regular languages are closed under complement, so $ \overline{L_2} $ is not necessarily regular.

CFLs are not closed under complement.

Therefore, $ \overline{L_2} $ may not be context-free and could be more complex.

Intersection of a regular language with a non-context-free language is not guaranteed to be context-free.

Conclusion:

$ L_1 - L_2 $ is not necessarily a context-free language.

Final Answer:

Option D is the correct choice because the set difference of a regular language and a context-free language is not necessarily context-free.

Answer: Option D

content: Let $$\left\langle M \right\rangle $$ denote an encoding of an automation M. Suppose that ∑ = {0, 1}. Which of the following languages is/are NOT recursive?

options.content: L = { $$\left\langle M \right\rangle $$ | M is a PDA such that L(M) = ∑*}
L = { $$\left\langle M \right\rangle $$ | M is a DFA such that L(M) = Φ}
L = { $$\left\langle M \right\rangle $$ | M is a PDA such that L(M) = Φ}
L = { $$\left\langle M \right\rangle $$ | M is a DFA such that L(M) = ∑*}

explanation:
To determine which of the given languages are not recursive (i.e., undecidable), we'll analyze each option individually.

Background Concepts

Recursive (Decidable) Languages: A language is recursive if there exists a Turing machine that will always halt and accept or reject any given string.

Pushdown Automaton (PDA): A computational model that recognizes context-free languages.

Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA): A computational model that recognizes regular languages.

Emptiness Problem: Determining whether the language recognized by an automaton is empty.

Universality Problem: Determining whether the language recognized by an automaton includes all possible strings over the alphabet $ \Sigma $.

Option Analysis

Option A:

$ L = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid M \text{ is a PDA such that } L(M) = \Sigma^* \} $

Problem: Determine if a PDA accepts all strings over $ \Sigma = \{0,1\} $.

Universality Problem for PDA:

Undecidable: The universality problem for PDAs is undecidable because context-free languages are not closed under complement.

Reason: There's no general algorithm to determine whether $ L(M) = \Sigma^* $ for a PDA $ M $.

Conclusion: Option A is not recursive.

Option B:

$ L = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid M \text{ is a DFA such that } L(M) = \emptyset \} $

Problem: Determine if a DFA accepts no strings.

Emptiness Problem for DFA:

Decidable: For DFAs, the emptiness problem is decidable.

Reason: We can perform a reachability analysis to see if any accepting state is reachable from the start state.

Conclusion: Option B is recursive.

Option C:

$ L = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid M \text{ is a PDA such that } L(M) = \emptyset \} $

Problem: Determine if a PDA accepts no strings.

Emptiness Problem for PDA:

Decidable: The emptiness problem for PDAs is decidable.

Reason: We can construct a context-free grammar equivalent to the PDA and test for emptiness, which is a decidable problem.

Conclusion: Option C is recursive.

Option D:

$ L = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid M \text{ is a DFA such that } L(M) = \Sigma^* \} $

Problem: Determine if a DFA accepts all strings over $ \Sigma $.

Universality Problem for DFA:

Decidable: The universality problem for DFAs is decidable.

Reason: We can construct the complement of the DFA (by swapping accepting and non-accepting states) and check for emptiness.

Conclusion: Option D is recursive.

Final Answer

The language in Option A is not recursive.

Answer: Option A

content:
Which of the following languages is generated by the given grammar?
$$$S \to aS|bS|\varepsilon $$$
explanation:
To determine which language is generated by the given grammar:

$ S \to aS \mid bS \mid \varepsilon $

We need to analyze the productions and understand the strings that can be derived from the start symbol $ S $.

Understanding the Grammar

Productions:

$ S \to aS $

$ S \to bS $

$ S \to \varepsilon $

Interpretation:

The grammar allows $ S $ to be replaced by:

An 'a' followed by $ S $.

A 'b' followed by $ S $.

The empty string $ \varepsilon $.

Implications:

This recursive definition means we can generate any string composed of any combination of 'a's and 'b's, in any order, including the empty string.

The recursion continues until $ S $ is eventually replaced by $ \varepsilon $, terminating the derivation.

Generating Strings

Examples of derivations:

Empty String ($ \varepsilon $)

$ S \to \varepsilon $

Single Characters

'a'

$ S \to aS \to a\varepsilon = a $

'b'

$ S \to bS \to b\varepsilon = b $

Multiple Characters

'ab'

$ S \to aS \to abS \to ab\varepsilon = ab $

'ba'

$ S \to bS \to baS \to ba\varepsilon = ba $

'abba'

$ S \to aS \to abS \to abbS \to abbaS \to abba\varepsilon = abba $

Any combination of 'a's and 'b's:

Since we can choose either 'a' or 'b' at each step, we can generate strings like 'aab', 'bab', 'baba', 'aaabbb', etc.

Language Generated

The grammar generates all possible strings over the alphabet $ \{ a, b \} $, including the empty string.

This set is represented as:

$ L = \{ a, b \}^* $

Where:

$ \{ a, b \} $ is the alphabet.

$ ^* $ denotes the Kleene star, representing zero or more occurrences of the alphabet symbols.

Analyzing Options

Let's compare the language generated with the given options.

Option A:

$ \left\{ a^n b^m \mid n, m \geq 0 \right\} $

Strings of a's followed by b's.

Examples: 'aabbb', 'aa', 'bbb', 'ab'.

Limitation: Does not include strings where 'a's and 'b's are intermixed, like 'aba', 'baab'.

Conclusion: Incorrect, as our grammar can generate strings not in this set.

Option B:

$ \left\{ w \in \{ a, b \}^* \mid \text{number of } a\text{'s} = \text{number of } b\text{'s} \right\} $

Strings with equal numbers of 'a's and 'b's.

Examples: 'ab', 'aabb', 'abab'.

Limitation: Does not include strings with unequal numbers of 'a's and 'b's, like 'aa', 'b', 'aab'.

Conclusion: Incorrect, as our grammar can generate strings not in this set.

Option C:

$ \left\{ a^n \mid n \geq 0 \right\} \cup \left\{ b^n \mid n \geq 0 \right\} \cup \left\{ a^n b^n \mid n \geq 0 \right\} $

Strings of only 'a's, only 'b's, or equal numbers of 'a's followed by 'b's.

Examples: 'aaa', 'bbb', 'aabb'.

Limitation: Does not include strings where 'a's and 'b's are intermixed or where counts are unequal, like 'aba', 'aabbb'.

Conclusion: Incorrect, as our grammar can generate strings not in this set.

Option D:

$ \{ a, b \}^* $

All possible strings over the alphabet $ \{ a, b \} $, including the empty string.

Examples: '', 'a', 'b', 'ab', 'ba', 'aba', 'bab', 'aaabbb', etc.

Conclusion: Correct, this matches the language generated by the grammar.

Final Answer

The language generated by the grammar $ S \to aS \mid bS \mid \varepsilon $ is $ \{ a, b \}^* $, which includes all possible strings over the alphabet $ \{ a, b \} $, including the empty string.

Answer: Option D

content:
Consider the following context-free grammar $G$ :

$$ \begin{aligned} & S \rightarrow a b a A B A b b a \\ & A \rightarrow a a B B A b \mid b B a b a a \\ & B \rightarrow a B b \mid a b \end{aligned} $$

In the above grammar, $S$ is the start symbol, $a$ and $b$ are terminal symbols, and $A$ and $B$ are non-terminal symbols.

Let $L(G)$ be the language generated by the grammar $G$. For a string $s \in L(G)$, let $n_1(s)$ be the number of a's in $s$ and $n_2(s)$ be the number of b's in $s$.

Which of the following statements is/are true?

options.content: There is a string $s \in L(G)$ such that $n_1(s)
There is a string $s \in L(G)$ such that $n_1(s)>2 n_2(s)$

explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & S \rightarrow a b a A B A b b a \\ & A \rightarrow a a B B A b \mid b B a b a a \\ & B \rightarrow a B b \mid a b \end{aligned} $$

Let us count the number of $a$'s and $b$'s produced by each non-terminal.

For $B$, the rules are $B \rightarrow aBb$ or $B \rightarrow ab$. In both cases, every step adds one $a$ and one $b$. So, in any string generated by $B$, the number of $a$'s and $b$'s is equal.

Now look at $A$. In the rule $A \rightarrow aaBBA b$, the fixed part contributes two $a$'s and one $b$, and each $B$ contributes equal numbers of $a$'s and $b$'s. So this rule always keeps the number of $a$'s at least as large as the number of $b$'s.

In the rule $A \rightarrow bBabaa$, the fixed part contributes three $a$'s and two $b$'s, and $B$ again contributes equal numbers of $a$'s and $b$'s. So here also, the number of $a$'s is at least the number of $b$'s.

Hence, every string generated by $A$ satisfies $n_1(s) \geq n_2(s)$.

Now consider the start production $S \rightarrow abaABA bba$. The fixed terminals in this rule give equal numbers of $a$'s and $b$'s, and both $A$ and $B$ produce strings in which the number of $a$'s is at least the number of $b$'s, with $B$ giving equal numbers. Therefore, for every string $s \in L(G)$, we get

$$ \therefore \quad n_1(s) \geq n_2(s) $$

Next, check the upper bound. In every production, the number of $a$'s never becomes more than twice the number of $b$'s. This is true for $B$ because it always gives equal numbers of $a$'s and $b$'s. It is also true for both productions of $A$, and therefore remains true for strings generated from $S$.

So, for every string $s \in L(G)$,

$$ n_1(s) \leq 2 n_2(s) $$

Therefore, the true statements are exactly those matching

$$ n_1(s) \geq n_2(s) \quad \text { and } \quad n_1(s) \leq 2 n_2(s) $$

Correct options is (b), and (d).

content:
Consider the following two languages over the alphabet $\{a, b, c\}$, where $m$ and $n$ are natural numbers.

$$\begin{aligned} & L_1=\left\{a^m b^m c^{m+n} \mid m, n \geq 1\right\} \\ & L_2=\left\{a^m b^n c^{m+n} \mid m, n \geq 1\right\} \end{aligned}$$

Which ONE of the following statements is CORRECT?

options.content: $L_1$ is a context-free language but $L_2$ is not a context-free language.
$L_1$ is not a context-free language but $L_2$ is a context-free language.

explanation:
Consider the two languages defined over the alphabet $\{a, b, c\}$:

$L_1 = \{a^m b^m c^{m+n} \mid m, n \geq 1\}$

$L_2 = \{a^m b^n c^{m+n} \mid m, n \geq 1\}$

The statement that is correct can be understood through the following analysis:

For $L_1$:

The language requires that the number of $a$'s and $b$'s must be equal ($a^m b^m$), and the number of $c$'s must be greater than or equal to the number of $a$'s ($c^{m+n}$).

This involves two simultaneous conditions or comparisons: one for equality between the number of $a$'s and $b$'s, and another for comparing the number of $a$'s to $c$'s.

Context-Free Grammars (CFGs) cannot handle two conditions at once, which require comparison of counts, thus, $L_1$ cannot be generated by a context-free grammar.

Therefore, $L_1$ is not a context-free language.

For $L_2$:

The language structure $\{a^m b^n c^{m+n}\}$ allows for:

Pushing $a$'s onto a stack.

Pushing $b$'s onto another stack or different part of a stack until $c$ appears.

Matching $c$'s with $a$'s and $b$'s as they pop off the stack.

This behavior can be captured using a Pushdown Automaton (PDA), which aligns with how context-free languages are processed.

Thus, $L_2$ is a context-free language.

In summary:

$L_1$ is not a context-free language.

$L_2$ is a context-free language.

Consider a context-free grammar $G$ with the following 3 rules.

$S \rightarrow aS, \ S \rightarrow aSbS, S \rightarrow c$

Let $w \in L(G)$.

Let $n_a(w)$, $n_b(w)$, $n_c(w)$ denote the number of times $a$, $b$, $c$ occur in $w$, respectively. Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?
explanation:
Consider the language $ L $ generated by $ G $: $ L = {c, ac, aac, acbc, aacbaac, acbaacba} $

(A) This option is not necessarily true. For example, in the string "acbc", the number of $ a $'s (1) and $ b $'s (1) are equal. Thus, it's not always true that $ n_a(w) $ is greater than $ n_b(w) $.

(B) Valid for all strings in the language. For instance, in "aacbc", $ n_a(w) = 2 $, $ n_c(w) - 2 = 1 $. Here, $ 2 > (1 - 2) $. This holds true for any string in the language.

(C) True for any string in the language. For example, in "aacbaac", $ n_c(w) = 2 $ and $ n_b(w) = 1 $. Thus, $ n_c(w) = n_b(w) + 1 $.

(D) False. Consider "ac", $ n_c(w) = 1 $ and $ n_b(w) = 0 $. Here, $ 1 \neq 0 \times 2 $. Another example, "acbaacba", $ n_c(w) = 3 $ and $ n_b(w) = 2 $. Here, $ 3 \neq 2 \times 2 $.

Hence, the correct options are (B) and (C).

content: Let G = (V, Σ, S, P) be a context-free grammar in Chomsky Normal Form with Σ = { a, b, c } and V containing 10 variable symbols including the start symbol S. The string w = a30b30c30 is derivable from S. The number of steps (application of rules) in the derivation S ⟹ w is _______
explanation:
In a context-free grammar in Chomsky Normal Form (CNF), every production rule is of the form:

  • $$A \rightarrow BC$$
  • or $$A \rightarrow a$$

where $$A, B, C$$ are variables and $$a$$ is a terminal symbol from the alphabet $$\Sigma$$. Given this, the derivation process can be outlined as follows:

1. Start with the start variable $$S$$ and derive a chain of variables. 2. Each variable can be expanded according to the production rules until all the variables are replaced by terminals.

For the given string $$w = a^{30}b^{30}c^{30}$$, we need to count the number of steps to derive this string from the start symbol $$S$$. In CNF, any derivation of a string of length $$n$$ involves $$2n-1$$ steps. This includes:

  1. Generating the correct tree shape consisting of variables, which takes $$n-1$$ steps if each expansion introduces exactly two new variables in the intermediate steps.
  2. Replacing variables with terminal symbols, which takes an additional $$n$$ steps (each variable needs to be replaced by a terminal).

Thus, if the length of the string is $$|w|$$, the total number of steps is:

$$2|w| - 1$$

For the string $$w = a^{30}b^{30}c^{30}$$, the length $$|w|$$ is given by:

$$|w| = 30 + 30 + 30 = 90$$

Therefore, the number of steps in the derivation $$S \Rightarrow w$$ is:

$$2 \cdot 90 - 1 = 179$$

Thus, the number of steps (application of rules) in the derivation $$S \Rightarrow w$$ is 179.

For a string w, we define wR to be the reverse of w. For example, if w = 01101 then wR = 10110.

Which of the following languages is/are context-free?

options.content: {wxwRxR | w, x ∈ {0, 1}*}
{wwRxxR | w, x ∈ {0, 1}*}
{wxwR | w, x ∈ {0, 1}*}
{wxxRwR | w, x ∈ {0, 1}*}

explanation:
If the language is accepted by PDA, then it is CFL.

Consider each option :

Option (A) : $\left\{w x w^R x^R \mid w, x \in\{0,1\}^*\right\}$

Let w is pushed into stack to match with $w^R$, and then $x$ is pushed into stack to match with $x^R$.

Now to match w with $w^R, w$ should be at the top of stack but the top of the stack contains $x$.

So PDA cannot be designed, it is not CFL.

Option (B) : $\left\{w w^R x x^R \mid w, x \in\{0,1\}^*\right\}$

This language can be split as

$$ \begin{aligned} L_1 & =\left\{w w^R \mid w \in\{0,1\}^*\right\} \\ L_2 & =\left\{x x^R \mid x \in\{0,1\}^*\right\} \text { such that } L=L_1 \cdot L_2 \end{aligned} $$

Since $L_1$ is CFL, $L_2$ is CFL and CFLs are closed under concatenation therefore, given language is CFL.

Option (C) : $\left\{w x w^R \mid w, x \in\{0,1\}^*\right\}$

This language can be written as $0(0+1)^* 0+1(0+1)^* 1$ which is regular, so it is CFL too.

Option (D) : $\left\{w x x^R w^R \mid w, x \in\{0,1\}^*\right\}$

PDA can be constructed for this language, first push $w$ and $x$ into the stack then to match with $x^R$, pop $x$; later to match with $w^R$ pop $w$. Hence this language is CFL too, Hence the correct option are (B), (C) and (D).

In a pushdown automaton P = (Q, ∑, Γ, δ, q0, F), a transition of the form,

where p, q ∈ Q, a ∈ Σ ∪ {ϵ}, and X, Y ∈ Γ ∪ {ϵ}, represents

(q, Y) ∈ δ(p, a, X).

Consider the following pushdown automaton over the input alphabet ∑ = {a, b} and stack alphabet Γ = {#, A}.


The number of strings of length 100 accepted by the above pushdown automaton is ______
explanation:
Given :

PDA over the input alphabet $\Sigma=\{a, b\}$

And stack alphabet $\Gamma=\{\#, A\}$

PDAs can accept strings in two ways

By emptying the stack.

By reaching the final state.

In the given DFA, we can observe that in the transition from initial state $q_0 \rightarrow q_1$; # is pushed into the stack and is not popped out on any subsequent state.

So, we can conclude that the PDA does not accept by emptying the stack.

Now, in state $q_1$, only alphabet a is accepted and in state $q_2$, only alphabet b is accepted. This implies that the language is of the form $a^p b^q$. Also every time when a is accepted A is pushed into stack $(\varepsilon \rightarrow A)$ and when b is accepted A is popped out of stack $(A \rightarrow \varepsilon)$

To reach final state, we need at least one A to make the transition, which implies that the number of A's pushed is atleast one more than the number of A's popped.

Therefore, language accepted by the PDA is

$$ L=\left\{a^p b^q \mid p \geq q+1\right\} $$

In the question, it is given that

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & \text { Length of string }=100 \\\\ \Rightarrow & p+q=100 \end{array} $$

So the possible values, in the form $(p, q)$ are

$$ \begin{aligned} & \{(100,0),(99,1), \ldots \ldots \ldots .(51,49)\} \\\\ \Rightarrow & \text { Number of such strings }=50 \end{aligned} $$

Hence the correct answer is 50 .

explanation:
Consider each option one by one :

Option (A) : $\left\{w w^R \mid w \in\{a, b\}^*\right\}$

Only one comparison is performed therefore, it is CFL.

Option (B) : $\left\{w a^n b^n w^R \mid w \in\{a, b\}^*, n \geq 0\right\}$

Since when comparison has to be made between $w$ and $w^R, a^n$ will be on top of stack and comparison will not be possible. Therefore, it is not CFL.

$$ \text { Option (D) : }\left\{a^n b^i \mid i \in\{n, 3 n, 5 n\}^*, n \geq 0\right\} $$

Given language can be re-written as

Since it is union of three CFLs and CFLs are closed under union.

Therefore, it is CFL.

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content:
Consider the following languages:

$$\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm I}.\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ $$\left\{ {{a^m}{b^n}{c^p}{d^q}} \right.|m + p = n + q,$$ where $$\left. {m,n,p,q \ge 0} \right\}$$
$$\,\,\,\,\,\,{\rm II}.\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ $$\left\{ {{a^m}{b^n}{c^p}{d^q}} \right.|m = n$$ and $$p=q,$$ where $$\left. {m,n,p,q \ge 0} \right\}$$
$$\,\,\,\,{\rm III}.\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ $$\left\{ {{a^m}{b^n}{c^p}{d^q}} \right.|m = n = p$$ and $$p \ne q,$$ where $$\left. {m,n,p,q \ge 0} \right\}$$
$$\,\,\,\,{\rm IV}.\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$$ $$\left\{ {{a^m}{b^n}{c^p}{d^q}} \right.|mn = p + q,$$ where $$\left. {m,n,p,q \ge 0} \right\}$$

Which of the languages above are context-free?
explanation:
Definitions and Concepts:

Context-Free Languages (CFLs):

Languages that can be generated by context-free grammars (CFGs).

Can be recognized by pushdown automata (PDAs).

CFLs are closed under union, concatenation, and Kleene star, but not closed under intersection or complement.

Pushdown Automata (PDA):

Automata with a stack as memory.

Can keep track of one type of count (e.g., matching numbers of symbols).

Non-Context-Free Languages:

Languages that cannot be recognized by PDAs.

Examples include languages that require matching counts of more than two types of symbols simultaneously (e.g., $ \{ a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0 \} $).

Pumping Lemma for CFLs:

A tool used to prove that certain languages are not context-free by showing that they cannot be "pumped" according to the lemma's conditions.

Analyzing Each Language:

Language I:

$ L_1 = \{ a^m b^n c^p d^q \mid m + p = n + q, \ m,n,p,q \geq 0 \} $

Constraint: The total number of $ a $'s and $ c $'s equals the total number of $ b $'s and $ d $'s.

Analysis:

Since the symbols appear in a fixed order ( $ a^m b^n c^p d^q $ ), we can design a PDA to recognize $ L_1 $:

Push $ a $'s and $ c $'s onto the stack:

For each $ a $ or $ c $ read, push a symbol onto the stack.

Pop for $ b $'s and $ d $'s:

For each $ b $ or $ d $ read, pop a symbol from the stack.

Accept if the stack is empty at the end:

Ensures $ m + p = n + q $.

Conclusion: $ L_1 $ is context-free.

Language II:

$ L_2 = \{ a^m b^n c^p d^q \mid m = n \text{ and } p = q, \ m,n,p,q \geq 0 \} $

Constraints:

$ m = n $: Number of $ a $'s equals number of $ b $'s.

$ p = q $: Number of $ c $'s equals number of $ d $'s.

Analysis:

We can design a PDA that uses the stack in two phases:

Match $ a $'s and $ b $'s:

Push a symbol for each $ a $.

Pop a symbol for each $ b $.

Ensure the stack is empty after $ b $'s.

Match $ c $'s and $ d $'s:

Reuse the stack.

Push a symbol for each $ c $.

Pop a symbol for each $ d $.

Ensure the stack is empty at the end.

Conclusion: $ L_2 $ is context-free.

Language III:

$ L_3 = \{ a^m b^n c^p d^q \mid m = n = p \text{ and } p \ne q, \ m,n,p,q \geq 0 \} $

Constraints:

$ m = n = p $: Number of $ a $'s, $ b $'s, and $ c $'s are equal.

$ p \ne q $: Number of $ d $'s is different.

Analysis:

Matching three counts simultaneously ($ m = n = p $) is similar to the language $ \{ a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0 \} $, which is not context-free.

PDAs cannot compare and ensure equality among more than two types of symbols.

Conclusion: $ L_3 $ is not context-free.

Language IV:

$ L_4 = \{ a^m b^n c^p d^q \mid m \cdot n = p + q, \ m,n,p,q \geq 0 \} $

Constraint: Product of $ m $ and $ n $ equals the sum of $ p $ and $ q $.

Analysis:

The condition $ m \cdot n = p + q $ involves multiplication and addition of counts, which a PDA cannot compute.

There is no standard PDA mechanism to handle multiplication of variables.

Conclusion: $ L_4 $ is not context-free.

Final Answer:

Context-Free Languages: $ L_1 $ and $ L_2 $

Non-Context-Free Languages: $ L_3 $ and $ L_4 $

Therefore, the correct option is:

Answer: Option B

Consider the following context-free grammars:
$$\eqalign{ & {G_1}:\,\,\,\,\,S \to aS|B,\,\,B \to b|bB \cr & {G_2}:\,\,\,\,\,S \to aA|bB,\,\,A \to aA|B|\varepsilon ,\,\,B \to bB|\varepsilon \cr} $$

Which one of the following pairs of languages is generated by $${G_1}$$ and $${G_2}$$, respectively?

Which of the following languages are context-free? 
$$$\eqalign{
  & {L_1} = \left\{ {{a^m}{b^n}{a^n}{b^m}|m,n \ge 1} \right\}  \cr 
  & {L_2} = \left\{ {{a^m}{b^n}{a^m}{b^n}|m,n \ge 1} \right\}  \cr 
  & {L_3} = \left\{ {{a^m}{b^n}|m = 2n + 1} \right\} \cr} $$$
explanation:
Understanding Context-Free Languages (CFLs) :

CFLs are languages that can be generated by context-free grammars (CFGs) or recognized by pushdown automata (PDAs).

PDAs have a single stack and are capable of comparing counts of symbols by pushing and popping operations.

PDAs can match counts between two types of symbols but have limitations when more complex counting is involved.

Analyzing Each Language :

Language $ L_1 $ :

$ L_1 = \{ a^m b^n a^n b^m \mid m, n \geq 1 \} $

Structure :

The string has :

$ a^m $: $ m $ occurrences of 'a'.

$ b^n $: $ n $ occurrences of 'b'.

$ a^n $: $ n $ occurrences of 'a'.

$ b^m $: $ m $ occurrences of 'b'.

Constraints :

The number of 'a's in the first part equals the number of 'b's in the last part ($ m $).

The number of 'b's in the second part equals the number of 'a's in the third part ($ n $).

PDA Construction:

Read $ a^m $ :

For each 'a' read, push symbol 'X' onto the stack.

Read $ b^n $ :

For each 'b' read, push symbol 'Y' onto the stack.

Read $ a^n $ :

For each 'a' read, pop a 'Y' from the stack.

Reject if no 'Y' is available (ensures $ n $ 'a's match $ n $ 'b's).

Read $ b^m $ :

For each 'b' read, pop an 'X' from the stack.

Reject if no 'X' is available (ensures $ m $ 'b's match $ m $ 'a's).

Accept if the stack is empty at the end.

Conclusion for $ L_1 $ :

The PDA can recognize $ L_1 $, so $ L_1 $ is context-free.

Language $ L_2 $ :

$ L_2 = \{ a^m b^n a^m b^n \mid m, n \geq 1 \} $

Structure :

The string has :

$ a^m $: $ m $ occurrences of 'a'.

$ b^n $: $ n $ occurrences of 'b'.

$ a^m $: $ m $ occurrences of 'a'.

$ b^n $: $ n $ occurrences of 'b'.

Constraints:

The first and third parts have the same number of 'a's ($ m $).

The second and fourth parts have the same number of 'b's ($ n $).

Analysis :

Challenge :

Matching the first $ a^m $ with the third $ a^m $ requires comparing counts that are separated by $ b^n $.

Using a single stack, we cannot access the first $ m $ 'a's count after pushing $ n $ 'b's onto the stack.

Conclusion :

A PDA cannot recognize $ L_2 $ due to stack limitations.

$ L_2 $ is not context-free.

Language $ L_3 $ :

$ L_3 = \{ a^m b^n \mid m = 2n + 1 \} $

Structure :

The string has $ m $ 'a's followed by $ n $ 'b's.

Constraint :

The number of 'a's is exactly $ 2n + 1 $.

PDA Construction:

Read $ a^{2n+1} $:

For each 'a' read, push symbol 'X' onto the stack.

Read $ b^n $:

For each 'b' read, pop two 'X's from the stack.

Reject if fewer than two 'X's are available (ensures $ m = 2n + 1 $).

After reading all 'b's:

Check if exactly one 'X' remains on the stack.

Accept if true; otherwise, reject.

Conclusion for $ L_3 $:

The PDA can recognize $ L_3 $, so $ L_3 $ is context-free.

Final Answer:

$ L_1 $ is context-free.

$ L_2 $ is not context-free.

$ L_3 $ is context-free.

Therefore, the languages that are context-free are $ L_1 $ and $ L_3 $ only.

Answer: Option B

explanation:
Given Languages :

$ L_1 = \{ 0^p1^q \mid p, q \in N \} $

Strings of any number of 0s followed by any number of 1s.

$ L_2 = \{ 0^p1^q \mid p, q \in N \text{ and } p = q \} $

Strings where the number of 0s equals the number of 1s.

$ L_3 = \{ 0^p1^q0^r \mid p, q, r \in N \text{ and } p = q = r \} $

Strings where the number of 0s, 1s, and 0s again are all equal.

Analyzing Each Statement:

Option A: PDAs can be used to recognize $ L_1 $ and $ L_2 $.

$ L_1 $:

Regular expression: $ 0^*1^* $

Regular language, hence can be recognized by a finite automaton (a PDA can also recognize it).

$ L_2 $:

Language of the form $ 0^n1^n $

Context-free language, can be recognized by a Pushdown Automaton (PDA).

Conclusion: True

Option B: $ L_1 $ is a regular language.

$ L_1 $:

As established, it can be represented by the regular expression $ 0^*1^* $.

Regular language.

Conclusion: True

Option C: All three languages are context-free.

$ L_1 $: Regular (hence context-free).

$ L_2 $: Context-free.

$ L_3 $:

Language of the form $ 0^n1^n0^n $.

Not context-free. This is a well-known non-context-free language because context-free grammars cannot enforce equal counts of three different symbols.

Conclusion: False

Option D: Turing machines can be used to recognize all the languages.

Turing Machines:

Capable of recognizing all recursively enumerable languages, which include regular, context-free, and some non-context-free languages.

$ L_3 $:

Although not context-free, $ L_3 $ is recursively enumerable.

Conclusion: True

Final Answer:

Option A: True

Option B: True

Option C: False

Option D: True

Therefore, the statement that is not true is:

Answer: Option C

content: Which of the following languages over $$\left\{ {a,b,c} \right\}$$ is accepted by Deterministic push down automata?
explanation:
Consider each option one by one :

Option (A) : $\left\{w c w^R \mid w \in\{a, b\}^*\right\}$

$w c w^r$ can be realized using a deterministic pushdown automata because we know the center of palindrome which is $c$ here .

Option (B) : $\left\{w w^R \mid w \in\{a, b, c\}^*\right\}$

$w w^r$ can be realized using a non deterministic pushdown automata. Here we don't know the center of palindrome.

Option (C) : $\left\{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 0\right\}$

It is a context sensitive language.

Option (D) : $\{w \mid w$ is a palindrome over $\{a, b$, c}}

$\{w \mid w$ is a palindrome over $\{a, b, c\}\}$ is realized using non deterministic pushdown automata because we don't know the center of palindrome.

Hence the correct option is (A).
